title: Castanea tribuloides
taxonomy: Castanea tribuloides belongs to the family Fagaceae. This genus is part of the order Fagales. The family Fagaceae and order Fagales are part of the class Magnoliopsida, which is a subclass of the larger class Magnoliopsida. Furthermore, Magnoliopsida is a subclass within the division Magnoliophyta. The division Magnoliophyta is a major group within the kingdom Plantae.
uses: Castanea tribuloides, a lesser-known species of chestnut, is reported to have various medicinal properties. However, more research is needed to confirm its effectiveness in treating specific ailments. The plant's bark, leaves, and seeds have been traditionally used in folk medicine for their purported antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Nonetheless, its medicinal uses require further scientific verification and study.

side_effects: Castanea tribuloides is not a valid species name for a known plant. However, based on the information about Castanea sativa, the sweet chestnut, it is generally considered safe for consumption in moderate amounts. However, eating large quantities of the seeds or leaves can cause stomach upset, diarrhea, and allergic reactions in some individuals. Additionally, eating unripe or green chestnuts can release a compound called saponin, which can cause nausea, vomiting, and other gastrointestinal issues. Consuming large amounts of saponin can also cause kidney damage and other adverse effects in severe cases.
